steps.title: Spot the contrasting outcomes
steps.content: The passage begins by praising a success: historians have deduced the authorship of Publius and many related essays. Then it shifts to other pen names that remain as elusive as they once were — unsolved. The transition must bridge between two opposite outcomes: solved versus unsolved.

steps.title: Select the concessive transition
steps.content: We need a word that acknowledges the contrast: “Yes, we solved Publius, but these others remain elusive.” “Though” serves as a concessive transition, expressing this expected-versus-actual contrast—these names were perhaps expected to be solved (like Publius), yet they remain unsolved. The other choices fail to capture the opposition: “indeed” merely reinforces the elusiveness without contrasting it to the Publius success, “likewise” falsely suggests similarity between solved and unsolved cases, and “for example” incorrectly positions these names as instances of the solved ones. That matches choice B — B is correct.
